data.title: AMC Networks Chief Financial Officer Patrick O'Connell Resigns
data.description:
By Colin Kellaher

AMC Networks' chief financial officer, Patrick O'Connell, is leaving the television broadcasting and production company to pursue an opportunity outside of the media industry.

AMC on Thursday said O'Connell, who joined the New York company as finance chief in September 2022, will stay on through March and participate in its 2025 earnings call.

AMC said it has launched a search for O'Connell's successor.

Write to Colin Kellaher at colin.kellaher@wsj.com

data.title: Google Expands AI-Powered Search With Personalized Results Linked To Gmail And Photos
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - Google has announced that it's making its search results more personalized by introducing an AI Mode that syncs with users' Gmail and Google Photos accounts. This is part of their efforts to enhance the Personal Intelligence feature.

The update aims to make Gemini, which is Google's main AI model, function better as a personalized assistant by utilizing user data from various Google apps. The idea is that AI Mode will tap into emails, photos, and past activities to provide more relevant search results, so you won't have to go into great detail when you type in your queries.

Robby Stein, who is the VP of product for Google Search, mentioned that the goal is to turn Search into a more personalized experience by pulling insights from across Google services. For instance, if someone is planning a trip, the suggestions might be based on travel info in their Gmail and photos that reflect their interests. Similarly, shopping searches could consider past purchases and personal preferences.

This feature will be optional, and users can unlink apps whenever they want. Google also reassured that emails and photos accessed via AI Mode won't be used to train future models.

Currently, the feature is experimental and is rolling out in English for Google AI Pro and Ultra subscribers in the U.S., but it won't be available for enterprise and education accounts.

Copyright(c) 2026 RTTNews.com. All Rights Reserved

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: Elon Musk Is Sparring With Ryanair's CEO. It's Helping the Airline Stock. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

Far from hurting Ryanair stock, the spat between SpaceX and Tesla CEO Elon Musk and Michael O'Leary, the airline's chief executive, can help Ryanair's shares.

It's a reason for U.S. investors to look across the Atlantic for new airline investments.

Over the past few days, tempers of the two CEOs flared up after Ryanair played down the idea of using SpaceX's Starlink broadband service for its flights. O'Leary, who works hard to keep costs down, didn't want to add Starlink because it could potentially harm fuel efficiency. Musk called the view misinformed.

From there, name-calling started. Ryanair launched a big " idiot" seat sale, and Musk mused about buying the airline, calling O'Leary a " chimp" on Monday.

An American citizen buying a European airline wouldn't be allowed, but O'Leary encouraged Musk to buy shares as a good investment.

Wall Street agrees. JPMorgan analyst Harry Gowers increased his price target for Ryanair stock to 38 euros ($44.52) from EUR34.50 on Thursday, implying a gain of 30% from recent levels. Gowers rates shares Buy. Ryanair continues to have a cost advantage over European peers, he says.

Raymond James analyst Savanthi Syth noted healthy demand for European airlines in a recent report, saying she remains "constructive" on Ryanair stock. Syth rates Ryanair's American depositary receipts at Buy and has a target of $85 for the price.

Overall, 77% of analysts covering Ryanair shares rate them at Buy, according to FactSet. The average Buy-rating ratio for stocks in the S&P 500 is about 55%.

The ADRs, which trade under "RYAAY," closed up 0.7% at $70.92 on Thursday, while the S&P 500 and the Dow Jones Industrial Average both gained about 0.6%.

Through Thursday trading, shares have gained about 3% since the Musk-O'Leary conflict began. Musk isn't likely to buy the airline, but his suggestion that he might may have shone a light on Ryanair's cost advantage.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Ellevest's Emily Green: These Mistakes Could Cost You Your Female Wealth Management Clients — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Weld Royal

The financial services industry often talks about the great wealth transfer to millennials and Gen Z. But all that discussion may be missing the more immediate — and massive — shift happening right now. "Men die on average six to eight years before women," says Emily Green, head of wealth management at Ellevest, which has $1.2 billion in assets under management. This creates what she calls a "horizontal transfer" before wealth ever reaches adult children. "It's actually not the millennials that we keep talking about" who will control this money first, Green explains. It's widows.

On our latest The Way Forward: Next Generation podcast , Green talks about what many widows do shortly after inheriting money, and why that should worry financial advisors. "Over 70% of the time in the first year after their husband's death, they will fire their advisor...or leave the firm...that they were at," Green says. For an industry managing the largest intergenerational wealth transfer in history this represents a problem.

The simple mistakes driving women away. Green says that the loss of female clients in general often stems from simple errors. She recalls a client who moved tens of millions of dollars to Ellevest after her previous advisor, whom she had inherited from her parents, repeatedly called her husband about her accounts. Despite her complaints, the advisor continued to call her husband.

"She would tell them, 'Don't do this...This matters to me,'" Green recalls. When the client finally switched firms, Green anticipated someone difficult to please. Instead, Green describes someone who likes to talk about her investments. "She's just so thankful that we just talk to her."

Keeping a female client happy doesn't necessarily require having a woman advisor serve her, says Green. In fact, she Green cites a McKinsey study that found women weren't specifically seeking female advisors — they just wanted to be acknowledged. Green points to subtle but crucial details: Who do you make eye contact with in meetings? Whose name appears first in emails? Do you alternate, or always default to the husband? Who gets invited to social events?

"How you're addressing them, how you're engaging with them socially," Green says, matters to relationship retention.

Debunking the risk-averse myth. Equally important to relationship retention is understanding women's views on risk. Green talks about the commonly held assumption that women are risk- averse investors and points to Ellevest proprietary data challenging the assumption. During the market turmoil of 2020 and 2022, Ellevest's platform of tens of thousands of investors showed women maintaining steadier hands than their male counterparts, according to Green.

"In these rough market periods, people are calling and pulling out of the markets and they're terrified, but we didn't see that here," Green recalls. She said women wanted to understand market movements, but they weren't panic-selling. In other words, they were risk aware, not risk averse.

This distinction especially matters for how advisors communicate during volatility. Instead of assumptions about fear, Green advocates focusing questions on goals, such as, "What are you trying to achieve?" This approach proved effective for the Ellevest advisor even during downturns, particularly when combined with values-based investing that helps clients see their money's broader impact.

Practical steps for advisors. Green offers three specific recommendations for advisors seeking to better serve women: First, meet women where they are — including social media, where even clients with tens of millions follow advisor content. Second, consistently bring conversations back to individual goals rather than market performance. Third, examine your team's composition and ensure women have speaking roles in meetings, not just note-taking duties. "I'm not telling you women only want a female advisor, but I am telling you that she doesn't want to go on your website and see only men," Green says.

Write to advisor.editors@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Former US prosecutor Smith says Trump 'willfully broke' laws in bid to keep power
data.description:
Key points:
  • Smith testified before Republican-led House panel
  • Prosecutor told lawmakers Trump willfully broke laws
  • Republicans painted probe as politically biased

By Andrew Goudsward and Nolan D. McCaskill

Former U.S. Special Counsel Jack Smith, who unsuccessfully prosecuted President Donald Trump, told a House of Representatives panel on Thursday that Trump was "looking for ways to stay in power" following his defeat in the 2020 election as he confronted Republican criticism of his investigation.

Smith fielded questions from the Republican-controlled House Judiciary Committee about his two criminal cases, which he dropped after Trump won the 2024 presidential election. One case accused Trump of conspiring to overturn his defeat in the 2020 election, while the other accused him of unlawfully holding onto classified documents.

The hearing marked the first time the American public heard at length from Smith, whose historic prosecutions dominated Trump's years out of power and helped fuel the Republican president's quest for retribution since returning to office. Smith told the panel he expected Trump's Justice Department to try to bring criminal charges against him.

"President Trump was charged because the evidence established that he willfully broke the very laws that he took an oath to uphold," Smith told the House panel. "If asked whether to prosecute a former president based on the same facts today, I would do so, regardless of whether that president was a Democrat or a Republican."

After the hearing, Trump reiterated his calls for Smith to be prosecuted, writing on social media that he had "destroyed the lives of many innocent people."

REPUBLICANS ALLEGE BIAS

Republican lawmakers sought to discredit Smith's investigation and buttress Trump's claims that the probes were an abuse of the legal system. Republicans focused particular attention on Smith's decision to seek limited phone records from former House Speaker Kevin McCarthy and several Republican senators, along with court orders that barred lawmakers from being notified of the subpoenas.

Trump allies have argued that the records show Smith’s investigation was overzealous and aimed at the political opposition.

"It was always about politics," Representative Jim Jordan of Ohio, the Republican chair of the Judiciary Committee, said at the start of the hearing. "To get Donald Trump, they were willing to do just about anything."

Smith said the records were necessary to examine Trump’s efforts to pressure Republican lawmakers to block certification of the election. He said he had "grave concerns about obstruction of justice in this investigation, specifically with regards to Donald Trump."

Smith’s testimony focused primarily on the case that accused Trump of using false voter fraud claims to obstruct the certification of election results following his 2020 defeat to Democrat Joe Biden. He told lawmakers that Republican witnesses, especially those who informed Trump that his fraud claims were not true, would have formed the core of the case had it gone to trial.

"Our investigation revealed that Donald Trump was not looking for honest answers about whether there was fraud in the election," Smith said. "He was looking for ways to stay in power."

A federal judge has barred the Justice Department from disclosing many of the details surrounding Smith’s second case, which accused Trump of stashing highly sensitive government documents at his Mar-a-Lago residence following the end of his first term in 2021.

Trump pleaded not guilty to all charges and has repeatedly argued the charges were improperly aimed at damaging his 2024 campaign.

Neither case reached trial and Smith dropped them after Trump won reelection, citing a Justice Department policy against prosecuting a sitting president.

The Trump administration has fired dozens of Justice Department lawyers, FBI agents and staffers who worked on the investigations.

Democrats defended Smith as an apolitical career prosecutor who was guided by the evidence in building his cases against Trump.

Representative Jamie Raskin of Maryland, the top Democrat on the panel, said Trump has assailed Smith "not because you did anything wrong, but because you did everything right."

"You had the audacity to do your job," Raskin added.

Crawley Mandell, Chief Client Officer, sold 7,860 shares at weighted average prices ranging from $181.45 to $186.46, totaling $1,441,879. Following these sales, Mandell directly owns 68,788 shares.

data.title: US power grid faces stress test amid arctic chill, data center demand
data.description:
By Tim McLaughlin and Scott DiSavino

The U.S. power grid faces an elevated risk of disruption over the next several days as an Arctic blast and reduced gas supplies test the sturdiness of electric infrastructure vulnerable to the around-the-clock demands of data centers.

Beginning Friday, an extreme weather pattern of Arctic cold is expected to evolve and bring extremely low temperatures and heavy snow and ice, with the potential to create significant challenges for the electric grid through next week. That was the assessment given on Thursday by the North American Electric Reliability Corporation, the top regulator for power reliability in the United States.

Cold weather has already started to reduce gas supplies by freezing oil and gas wells and pipes in several Midwest and Great Plains states .

As a result, U.S. natural gas futures soared by a record 63% over the past three days to a six-week high on Thursday on forecasts of extreme cold over the next two weeks.

Demand next week is expected to approach last winter's record as homes and businesses crank up their heaters to escape a brutal cold snap, according to power grid operators, analysts and data from financial firm LSEG.

Meanwhile, winter electricity demand on the largest U.S. power grid - the PJM Interconnection - is expected to set an all-time record next week.

"Peak demand has the potential to exceed 130,000 MW for seven straight days next week, a winter streak that PJM has never experienced," the grid operator said on Thursday in a cautionary alert.

"Depending on temperatures, PJM could set a new all-time winter peak load on Jan. 27. And that cold could extend into early February, so PJM is taking additional precautions with its generation and transmission owners to prepare."

PJM serves 67 million people in 13 states and Washington, D.C. It has been struggling to keep up with booming demand from the proliferation of Big Tech's power-hungry data centers needed for the rapid expansion of artificial intelligence.

In recent years, regional grids, including PJM, have been caught flat-footed by extreme weather.

The worst episode happened in Texas in early 2021 when more than 200 people died as the state's main grid lost nearly 50% of its generation capacity. Millions of Texans were left without power, including the family of an 11-year-old boy who froze to death while sleeping in his bed.

The Electric Reliability Council of Texas (ERCOT), the state's main grid operator, was criticized in after-action reports for not making sure the system was properly winterized.

Since then, stricter state and federal rules have been implemented to require better winter readiness by utilities and grid operators. ERCOT, for example, plans to complete a minimum of 450 inspections this winter season (December–February). As of January 18, 240 inspections had been completed, ERCOT said.

But the gap between winter and summer peaks is narrowing, caused by rapid data center growth, said Philip Krein, a grid expert at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign.

That means utilities have less time to take power plants offline, for example, to perform maintenance.

"That makes the grid more vulnerable," Krein said. "The maintenance season is being squeezed like never before."

ERCOT and PJM both said they expected they will have enough generation to meet demand this winter.

However, ERCOT has forecast there's a slight chance - 1.18% - of a small, controlled blackout on the morning of January 31.

ERCOT CEO Pablo Vegas said on Thursday he was confident the Texas grid will be able to handle the demands of cold weather.

data.title: Capital One to buy fintech firm Brex in $5.15 billion deal
data.description:
Capital One Financial will acquire fintech firm Brex in a cash and stock deal valued at $5.15 billion, the consumer lender said on Thursday.

The deal will be carried out on a 50-50 cash-stock basis, it added.

The move comes at a time when dealmakers are expecting a rise in mergers and market debuts among retailers and consumer goods companies, after steep U.S. tariffs sidelined transaction activity across the sector in the first half of 2025.

Capital One also reported a rise in fourth-quarter profit, helped by a higher interest income from its credit card debt.

Its net income available to common stockholders came in at $2.06 billion, or $3.26 per share, during the quarter, compared with $1.02 billion, or $2.67 per share, a year earlier.

  • Company saw strong loan and deposit growth in Q4

  • Company repurchased 150,504 shares, enhancing shareholder value

Outlook

  • Company expects positive profitability trends to continue throughout 2026

Result Drivers

  • LOAN AND DEPOSIT GROWTH - Strong loan and deposit growth, particularly in Southwest and Southeast Louisiana, contributed to Q4 results

  • STABLE NET INTEREST MARGIN - Net interest margin remained stable at 3.71%, supporting consistent earnings

  • SHAREHOLDER VALUE - Stock repurchase program and increased book value per share enhanced shareholder value

data.title: Intel Corporation Reports Fourth-Quarter and Full-Year 2025 Financial Results
data.description:
Intel Corporation has released its financial results for the fourth quarter and full year of 2025. The report highlights a mixed performance with some areas of growth and others facing challenges. The company continues to navigate industry-wide supply shortages while making significant strides in advanced process technology and AI integration.

Financial Highlights

For the fourth quarter of 2025, Intel reported revenue of $13.7 billion, a 4% decrease year-over-year. The full-year revenue stood at $52.9 billion, remaining flat compared to the previous year. The deconsolidation of Altera in Q3 2025 was not adjusted in these comparisons. The GAAP earnings per share (EPS) for Q4 was $(0.12), while the non-GAAP EPS was $0.15. For the full year, GAAP EPS was $(0.06) and non-GAAP EPS was $0.42.

Business and Operational Highlights

Intel introduced the Intel® Core™ Ultra Series 3 processor family, marking its first AI PC platform built on the Intel 18A process technology. This new product is expected to power over 200 designs across various devices, including laptops, gaming handhelds, and industrial edge devices. Additionally, Intel and Cisco announced a collaboration on an integrated platform for distributed AI workloads, enhancing real-time AI inferencing at the edge.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

Intel completed the sale of $5.0 billion of its common stock to NVIDIA, bolstering its balance sheet and strategic flexibility. The company also made significant leadership appointments, including Cindy Stoddard as CIO, Robin Colwell as SVP of government affairs, and Annie Shea Weckesser as SVP and chief marketing and communications officer. Furthermore, Craig H. Barratt, Ph.D., was appointed as an independent member of the board of directors.

Management's Perspective

CEO Lip-Bu Tan emphasized the growing importance of CPUs in the AI era and highlighted the company's progress in building a new Intel. CFO David Zinsner noted that Q4 results exceeded expectations despite supply shortages and expressed optimism about improving supply levels in the coming quarters.

Future Outlook

For the first quarter of 2026, Intel forecasts revenue between $11.7 billion and $12.7 billion. The company expects a GAAP EPS of $(0.21) and a non-GAAP EPS of $0.00. The gross margin is projected to be 32.3% on a GAAP basis and 34.5% on a non-GAAP basis.

Original SEC Filing: INTEL CORP [ INTC ] - 8-K - Jan. 22, 2026

Disclaimer

data.title: Bank First Corporation Q4 2025 Financial Results
data.description:
Bank First Corporation, the holding company for Bank First, N.A., has released its financial results for the fourth quarter and full year ended December 31, 2025. The company reported strong financial performance, highlighted by significant growth in net income and earnings per share (EPS).

Financial Highlights

For the fourth quarter of 2025, Bank First reported a net income of $18.4 million, or $1.87 per share, compared to $17.5 million, or $1.75 per share, in the same period of the previous year. For the full year, net income was $71.5 million, or $7.23 per share, up from $65.6 million, or $6.50 per share, in 2024. The bank's net interest income (NII) for the quarter was $40.2 million, an increase from $35.6 million in the fourth quarter of 2024. The net interest margin (NIM) improved to 4.01% from 3.61% in the prior-year quarter.

Business and Operational Highlights

Bank First's total assets grew to $4.51 billion as of December 31, 2025, representing an annualized growth rate of 7.8% for the quarter. Total loans were $3.60 billion, with a slight contraction of $25.0 million during the fourth quarter, attributed to the exit of substandard relationships. Total deposits increased to $3.70 billion, with a notable annualized growth rate of 17.7% during the quarter.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

The acquisition of Centre 1 Bancorp, Inc., the parent company of First National Bank and Trust Company (FNBT), was a significant event for Bank First, closing on January 1, 2026. This acquisition is the largest in the bank's history, expanding its presence into new counties in Wisconsin and Illinois and adding Trust and Wealth Management services. Additionally, the bank declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.50 per share, an 11.1% increase over the prior quarter and prior-year fourth quarter.

Management's Perspective

Mike Molepske, Chairman and CEO of Bank First, expressed satisfaction with the company's performance, highlighting the 15% increase in annual earnings per share despite merger-related expenses. He emphasized the transformational impact of the FNBT acquisition, which significantly expands the bank's market presence and service offerings.

Future Outlook

Bank First's management remains optimistic about the future, focusing on integrating the FNBT acquisition and leveraging the expanded market presence to drive growth. The company aims to continue its strong financial performance and maintain its commitment to delivering value to shareholders.

Original SEC Filing: Bank First Corp [ BFC ] - 8-K - Jan. 22, 2026
